evals.expected_output: Skill triggers, runs Step 0 (get_deploy_token for identity), analyzes the project directory, detects Dockerfile/HTML, gathers deploy settings with smart defaults, presents confirmation, then proceeds with tar+curl upload workflow.
expectations: Calls get_deploy_token before asking the user for any information
Does NOT ask the user for their email address
Analyzes the current directory for Dockerfile, HTML files, .gitignore, and project type
Presents deploy settings (name, URL, size, port, auth, env vars) for confirmation before uploading
Defaults to huddle size and okta auth

evals.expected_output: Skill calls get_deploy_token first to establish identity, then calls list_deployed_apps. Presents results in a clean table format without raw JSON.
expectations: Calls get_deploy_token first to identify the user — does NOT ask for email
Calls list_deployed_apps after getting the token
Presents apps in a formatted table, not raw JSON
Shows app name, status, and URL at minimum

evals.prompt: my app sample-app keeps crashing, can you check whats going on?
evals.expected_output: Skill calls get_deploy_token, then get_status to check the app. If pods show CrashLoopBackOff or errors, auto-calls get_deploy_logs without being asked. Extracts the relevant error from logs and explains in plain language what's wrong and how to fix it.
expectations: Calls get_deploy_token first
Calls get_status for the named app
Auto-calls get_deploy_logs when status shows failure — does NOT wait for user to ask for logs
Extracts and highlights the actionable error from logs, not a full log dump
Explains the error in plain non-developer language
Suggests a specific fix or offers to fix it
Provides the dashboard URL

evals.prompt: I want to put my react project online so my team can see it. the code is in ~/projects/dashboard-prototype and I built it already, the output is in the dist folder
evals.expected_output: Skill recognizes this as a deploy request even though the user didn't say 'deploy' or 'hatch'. Detects the dist/ directory with index.html, creates a Dockerfile pointing at dist/, gathers settings, and deploys.
expectations: Triggers the skill even though user didn't mention 'hatch' or 'deploy' explicitly
Calls get_deploy_token for identity
Checks for Dockerfile first, then looks for index.html in dist/
Creates or confirms a Dockerfile that copies dist/ to nginx
Sets port to 80 for static site
Presents deploy settings for confirmation before proceeding

evals.expected_output: Skill calls get_deploy_token, confirms the user wants to destroy the app, then calls destroy_app. Reports success with the list of deleted resources.
expectations: Calls get_deploy_token first
Confirms with the user before destroying — doesn't delete immediately
Calls destroy_app with the correct app name
Reports which resources were deleted

evals.prompt: i tried to deploy my-new-app but it says something about a limit?? I have too many apps or something
evals.expected_output: Skill recognizes this as a quota/limit issue. Explains the 7-app limit in plain language. Lists the user's apps so they can choose which to delete. Offers to delete one and retry the deploy.
expectations: Calls get_deploy_token for identity
Explains the 7-app-per-owner limit in plain language
Calls list_deployed_apps to show the user's current apps
Offers to help delete an app to make room
Does NOT use jargon like 'HTTP 429' without explaining it

evals.prompt: add alice@dowjones.com as co-owner on sample-org-modeling and also give bob@dowjones.com access to use the app
evals.expected_output: Skill calls get_deploy_token, then add_coowner for alice and add_app_user for bob. Explains the difference between co-owner (can manage the app) and app user (can access the deployed URL).
expectations: Calls get_deploy_token first
Calls add_coowner for alice@dowjones.com
Calls add_app_user for bob@dowjones.com
Distinguishes between co-owner and app user roles in the response

evals.expected_output: When hatch MCP tools are not available, the skill detects this and auto-configures .mcp.json with the hatch server entry. Tells the user to approve the MCP connection.
expectations: Detects that hatch MCP tools are not available
Checks for .mcp.json in the project root
Creates or updates .mcp.json with the hatch server configuration
The hatch entry uses type 'http' and URL 'https://hatch.ai.build.pib.dowjones.io/'
Tells the user to approve the MCP connection and try again

evals.prompt: im getting an error when i deploy, it says something about secrets detected in my code. i have a .env file with my API keys, how do i deploy with those?
evals.expected_output: Skill explains that .env files are blocked for security. Offers to read the .env file and convert the values to the env_vars parameter, which stores them securely as K8s Secrets.
expectations: Explains why .env files are blocked — they would be pushed to GitHub
Offers to read the .env file and extract the values
Explains that env_vars are stored as Kubernetes Secrets, never in the repo
Shows how the values will be passed via the env_vars parameter
Does NOT tell the user to just remove the .env and figure it out themselves

evals.prompt: my app got deployed but when i open the URL it just shows a blank page or nginx 404. its a next.js app
evals.expected_output: Skill diagnoses this as a likely port or build output issue. Checks app status and logs. Explains that Next.js needs to be built (npm run build) and the server typically runs on port 3000, or if using static export, the output directory needs to be served correctly.
expectations: Calls get_deploy_token and get_status to check the app
Calls get_deploy_logs to check for runtime errors
Considers common Next.js issues: wrong port, missing build step, static vs server mode
Suggests checking the Dockerfile for correct build commands and port
Provides actionable steps to fix, not just a diagnosis

evals.prompt: whats the status of all my stuff
evals.expected_output: Skill interprets 'my stuff' as deployed apps. Calls get_deploy_token, then list_deployed_apps. Shows a summary table of all apps with their status.
expectations: Triggers the skill for this informal/vague request
Calls get_deploy_token first
Calls list_deployed_apps
Presents results clearly — not raw JSON

evals.prompt: the archive is 80MB and the upload is failing. i already excluded node_modules. what else can i do?
evals.expected_output: Skill helps diagnose the oversized archive. Suggests inspecting contents with tar tzf, looks for common culprits (.git, build outputs, media files, python venvs). Offers to check .gitignore for missing excludes.
expectations: Suggests running tar tzf to inspect archive contents
Lists common culprits beyond node_modules: .git, dist, build, .next, venv, __pycache__, media files
Offers to read .gitignore and generate missing --exclude flags
Mentions the 50MB limit
Does NOT just say 'exclude more files' — gives specific actionable items
